1: The bronze altar: 20 × 20 cubits square and 10 cubits high — the place of substitutionary sacrifice, the structural center of Israel's atonement system. Christ is 'the altar from which those who serve the tent have no right to eat' (Hebrews 13:10) — the sacrifice and the altar converged in one person, the one offering that rendered all others obsolete.
2: The bronze sea: 10 cubits diameter, 5 cubits high, 30 cubits circumference — a massive cleansing basin for priestly washing. Before anyone could approach the altar or enter the sanctuary, washing was required. Christ cleanses: 'he gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word' (Ephesians 5:25-26).
3: Under the rim of the bronze sea were two rows of gourds — the vegetation motifs of the tabernacle and the garden-of-Eden symbolism embedded in the temple's design. The original garden had water (Genesis 2:10); the temple sea perpetuates the water-of-life motif; Christ is 'the spring of water welling up to eternal life' (John 4:14).
4: The sea rested on twelve oxen — three facing each direction of the compass, supporting the basin. The twelve oxen = twelve tribes of Israel: the covenant people bearing the cleansing waters. In the new creation, the 'river of the water of life' flows from the throne of God and of the Lamb (Revelation 22:1-2) — not supported by twelve oxen but flowing from the enthroned Christ himself.
5: The sea held 3,000 baths — vastly exceeding daily need. The abundance of cleansing water: 'the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin' (1 John 1:7). The all-inclusive 'all sin' is the NT equivalent of the sea's excessive capacity — more than enough for every worshipper who approaches.
6: Ten lavers for washing what pertained to burnt offerings; the sea for the priests to wash in. The lavers cleanse the sacrifices; the sea cleanses the priests. Christ purifies both: his body (the true sacrifice) was 'holy, innocent, unstained' (Hebrews 7:26); and 'you are already clean because of the word I have spoken to you' (John 15:3).
7: Ten golden lampstands — five on the south side, five on the north — illuminate the interior. Christ is 'the light of the world' (John 8:12); in Revelation 1:12-13, John sees 'seven golden lampstands, and in the midst of the lampstands one like a son of man' — Christ walking among his churches who are themselves lampstands (Revelation 1:20), the light-bearing communities that reflect his light.
8: Ten tables and 100 golden basins. The tables hold the bread of the presence; the basins hold sacrificial blood. Both together constitute the covenant meal-system: presence (bread) through atonement (blood). Christ fulfills both at the Last Supper: 'this is my body' / 'this is my blood of the covenant' — the bread-and-cup that the temple's tables and basins anticipated.
9: The courts — great court and court of the priests — with their bronze-overlaid doors. The courts provide graduated access: outer court for Israel; inner court for priests. Christ removes this gradation: 'there is neither Jew nor Greek... for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28). In the new Jerusalem there are no restricted courts — 'its gates will never be shut by day' (Revelation 21:25).
10: The sea placed on the southeast side — the cleansing basin encountered early in the approach sequence. The gospel similarly presents cleansing before access: forgiveness precedes the feast of the presence; baptism marks entry into the covenant community.
11: Huram-abi made pots, shovels, and basins — completing all his work. 'He who began a good work in you will bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ' (Philippians 1:6). The Spirit-endowed builder of Christ's church completes what he starts — the eschatological building project will not be left unfinished.
12: The two pillars, the bowls, and the capitals — the structural and decorative coordination of pillars with their capitals represents the integration of foundation and crown. Christ is both foundation (1 Corinthians 3:11: 'no one can lay a foundation other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ') and crown (Revelation 19:12: 'on his head are many diadems').
13: 400 pomegranates for the two latticeworks — seed-bearing, fruitful ornaments. The pomegranate's seed-multiplicity is the type of the hundredfold harvest (Matthew 13:23); Christ is the true vine whose branches bear much fruit (John 15:5).
14: The ten bases and ten lavers — every component of the temple's cleansing system has a structural foundation. The gospel's cleansing is grounded in the objective historical foundation: Christ 'died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures' (1 Corinthians 15:3) — not a free-floating ritual but an act with a fixed historical and covenantal base.
15: The one sea and the twelve oxen under it — one source of cleansing, one covenant community bearing it. The body of Christ is similarly one body with many members (1 Corinthians 12:12-27), all sharing the one baptism (Ephesians 4:5).
16: Pots, shovels, forks — the practical instruments of the altar's daily work. These unglamorous tools are part of the temple's functional holiness. Christ's priestly ministry is similarly comprehensive: 'he is able to help those who are being tempted' (Hebrews 2:18) — no aspect of human need is too ordinary for his priestly attention.
17: The bronze vessels cast in the Jordan valley between Succoth and Zeredah — the same region where Jacob wrestled and the Jordan was crossed under Joshua. Sacred geography recurs: the place of crossing becomes the source of temple vessels, just as the Jordan of baptism (Matthew 3:13-17) is the place of the one whose sacrifice provides the temple's cleansing.
18: Solomon made all these things in great quantity such that the weight of the bronze was not determined. The abundance exceeds cataloguing — the same excess of provision seen in the feeding of five thousand (twelve baskets of leftovers) and the wine at Cana (six jars filled to the brim). Christ's provision for his people consistently exceeds the measure of need.
19: All the vessels in the temple — the golden altar, the tables for the bread of the presence. The two gold installations — altar of incense and tables of presence — represent the two primary forms of temple communion: prayer (incense) and provision (bread). Christ is the fulfillment of both: the one who ever lives to intercede (Hebrews 7:25) and the bread of life who gives himself for the world (John 6:51).
20: The lampstands with their lamps burning continually before the inner sanctuary — the perpetual light of divine presence. Christ promises unceasing light: 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness' (John 8:12); in the new Jerusalem, 'the Lord God will be their light, and they will reign forever and ever' (Revelation 22:5).
21: The flowers, lamps, and tongs of purest gold — every interior element contributing to a unified, holy space. The purity of material signifies the purity of the presence it serves. 'The city was pure gold, clear as glass' (Revelation 21:18) — and 'nothing unclean will ever enter it... but only those who are written in the Lamb's book of life' (Revelation 21:27).
22: The sockets of the temple doors — even the hinges are of gold. There is no diminishing of holiness at the threshold — the approach and the interior share the same quality of sacred presence. Christ is the door (John 10:7-9): the threshold-person who does not reduce the holiness of what lies beyond but himself constitutes the way into it.
